lesson: Wrap a shared counter's increment in `with threading.Lock():` so concurrent read-modify-write from multiple threads can't lose updates.
Use threading.Event with event.wait(timeout) inside a worker's loop condition so a setter thread can cleanly stop it instead of killing it.
Push a sentinel object through queue.Queue so the consumer thread knows to exit, instead of guessing when the producer is done.
Use threading.RLock (not Lock) when a locked method calls another locked method on the same object from the same thread, or it self-deadlocks.
Use Condition.wait_for(predicate) instead of a bare wait()+notify() to implement a bounded buffer safely against spurious wakeups and lost wakeups.
Wrap a resource pool access in `with threading.Semaphore(n):` to cap concurrent holders at n regardless of how many threads race for it.
Avoid multi-lock deadlock (transfer(a,b) vs transfer(b,a) racing) by always acquiring locks in a fixed global order, e.g. sorted by object id, never per-call argument order.

Don't trust the GIL for compound ops: `x = x + 1` is load/add/store, not one bytecode, so it can still race; insert time.sleep(0) between the read and write to force/prove the interleaving, then fix it with an explicit Lock.
